title: Genesis 9
subtitle: The Noahic Covenant; the Table of Ham

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — The Noahic Commission and the Sanctity of Blood

notes.note: MacArthur: The Noahic commission repeats the Adamic commission (1:28) but with modifications: the fear of man is placed on animals (v.2), and meat is now permitted for food (v.3). The post-Fall, post-Flood world requires adjusted provisions within the original creational framework.

MacArthur: Capital punishment for murder is grounded in the image of God: 'Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed, for in the image of God has God made mankind.' The image doctrine is the foundation of human dignity and the basis for justice.

heb.paragraph: "God blessed Noah and his sons" (v.1) deliberately echoes the creation blessing of 1:28: "God blessed them and said, 'Be fruitful and multiply.'" The Noahic covenant is a re-creation covenant — the blessing formula is the signal that the creation mandate is being re-issued. Every element of the first blessing reappears: fruitfulness, multiplication, dominion over animals. The Flood was not the end of creation but its purgation and renewal.

The new permission to eat meat (v.3) is immediately qualified by the prohibition on consuming blood (v.4: "you must not eat meat that has its lifeblood still in it"). Blood (dam) in biblical law carries the life of the creature (nefesh — soul/life, v.4) — to eat blood would be to consume life itself, which belongs to God. This is the theological foundation of all subsequent blood legislation: Leviticus 17:11 — "the life of a creature is in the blood."

The prohibition on murder grounds itself in the Imago Dei (v.6): "Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed; for in the image of God has God made mankind." The image of God, transmitted through the genealogy of ch.5, is the basis of human dignity and the ground of the legal prohibition on homicide. Capital justice is not revenge but the defence of the Imago: to murder a person is to attack the image-bearer of God.

hist.historical: The Noahic covenant (vv.8-17) is the most universal covenant in Scripture — it is made with "every living creature" (vv.10,12,15,16,17), not just with Noah or Israel. It is the covenant of common grace: God's commitment to maintain the conditions of life for all creatures until the final consummation. The rainbow sign is a natural phenomenon (light refracting through water) that God appropriates as a covenant sign — the same creative power that sent the flood now signs a covenant of non-destruction.
hist.context: The Noahic commission (vv.1-7) is the post-flood version of the creation commission (1:28-30). The similarities are deliberate: the blessing (be fruitful and multiply), the dominion over animals, the provision of food. But significant differences appear: fear and dread now characterise the human-animal relationship (v.2), animal flesh is now permitted for food (v.3), and the sanctity of human life requires capital punishment (v.6). The new creation is similar to the first but operates under different conditions.

refs.note: The mandate to “be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth” is repeated almost verbatim in 9:1 — Noah receives the Adamic commission fresh, making him a second Adam.
The Jerusalem Council requires Gentile believers to abstain from blood — carrying the Noahic prohibition (Gen 9:4) into the new covenant era as a universal moral law.
God swears his covenant faithfulness to Israel “as in the days of Noah” — the Noahic covenant is the template for understanding God’s unbreakable commitments.
A rainbow encircles the throne of God in John’s vision — the covenant sign of Genesis 9 frames the divine throne-room in eternity.
Paul grounds the state’s authority to bear the sword in the divine mandate of justice — rooted in the Noahic institution of Gen 9:6.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Noahic re-commission parallels the creation mandate of 1:28-30 but with significant modifications. Sarna: (1) Animals now fear humans — the pre-fall harmony of creation is replaced by a functional hierarchy maintained by fear. (2) Humans may now eat animals — the original vegetarian diet (1:29-30) is expanded. (3) Blood is prohibited — the life (nefesh) is in the blood (Lev 17:11), and blood must not be consumed. (4) Capital punishment is mandated — murder is an assault on the image of God.

Shofeich dam haAdam, baAdam damo yishafech — "whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed." Sarna notes the poetic structure: the sentence is a chiasm in Hebrew — A-B-A-B — shed/blood/man/man/blood/shed. The form mirrors the content: murder creates a reciprocal obligation. The theological grounding — "for in the image of God has God made mankind" — makes murder an assault not just on the victim but on God himself.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The resumption of the blessing "be fruitful and multiply" connects the post-flood world to the creation. But Alter notes the modifications: "The new world is a world of fear. The animals will fear humans; humans may eat animals. The vegetarian paradise of Genesis 1-2 is over — the world has been through the flood, and the relationship between humans and animals is different on the other side."

The capital punishment verse is given in the formal Hebrew legal style: chiastic structure, compressed phrasing, theological grounding. Alter: "The image of God as the basis for the prohibition of murder is the most radical possible grounding for the value of human life. Every human being, regardless of status or merit, bears the divine image — and therefore every human killing is an assault on the image in which God sees himself reflected."

Calvin on the post-flood permission to eat animals: "God now permits to men the use of flesh, which he had not done before. The reason was that, since the earth had been purged by the deluge, he thought it good to allow men greater licence." Calvin does not read this as a change in the moral order but a gracious accommodation to the post-fall, post-flood world.

Capital punishment is divinely mandated here and has never been revoked. Calvin: "We see how God arms the magistrate with the sword for the avenging of slaughter, in order that the sacred image of God should not be violated with impunity. For though God had offered his own image to be trampled upon in the person of men, yet now, to prevent violence, he threatens death to all who shall dare to shed innocent blood." The image of God grounds both the prohibition of murder and the sanction for it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the expanded diet (all moving creatures permitted for food) discusses the transition from the implied vegetarianism of 1:29-30. The note observes this is not a concession to fallen appetites but a new divine grant — God explicitly permits what was not previously permitted. The restriction on blood (v.4) is the single qualification on this permission.

The NET note on "by man shall his blood be shed" discusses whether this establishes capital punishment as a universal norm (natural law) or merely a post-flood arrangement. The note surveys the NT treatment (Rom 13:1-7; Rev 13:10) and concludes that the image-of-God grounding makes this a universal principle, not culturally relative.

MacArthur: The rainbow is a sign (Hebrew: 'ot) -- the same word used for the signs of the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenants. Signs are not magic but memorial -- they remind both parties of the covenant's terms. God says explicitly that the rainbow will be for him to remember his covenant (v.15).

MacArthur: The Noahic covenant is unconditional and universal -- it encompasses all living creatures on earth and requires nothing from Noah or humanity for its maintenance. It is God's unilateral commitment to preserve the created order through history until the final judgment.

heb.paragraph: The Noahic covenant (vv.9-17) is the first formal covenant in Scripture, using the word bĕrît for the first time in Genesis. God initiates it unilaterally — not "if you do X, I will do Y" but an unconditional divine commitment to sustain the created order. The covenant is universal: with Noah, his descendants (all humanity), and every living creature. It is the covenant of common grace that underlies all subsequent history, including Israel's particular covenant and the new covenant.

The rainbow (qešet) as covenant sign (vv.12-17) is a warfare bow hung in the clouds — weapons of war set aside as a peace signal. When God looks at the bow, he will "remember my everlasting covenant between God and all living creatures" (v.16). The sign is oriented primarily toward God's own remembering, not humanity's: it is a reminder built into the creation of divine commitment, permanently marking the sky with the shape of a covenant.

The Noahic covenant is characterised as bĕrît ʿôlām — "everlasting covenant" (v.16). This is the first of four such "everlasting covenants" in Genesis-Deuteronomy (also Abrahamic: 17:7; Sabbath: Exod 31:16; Levitical: Num 25:13). The ʿôlām qualifier signals permanence that transcends any political arrangement — it is a commitment as durable as creation itself, grounded in God's own character rather than in human performance.

hist.historical: The rainbow covenant is unique among biblical covenants in being entirely unconditional and entirely universal. There are no human obligations attached — God simply commits himself. The covenant is with "all life" (vv.10,12,15,16,17), not just with humanity. This is the covenant of common grace — the theological term for God's universal provision for all creatures, regardless of their relationship to him. It is the basis for the regular seasons of 8:22 and the conditions for all subsequent human history.
hist.context: The covenant sign of the rainbow is striking in its direction: it is not a sign for humanity to observe primarily but for God. "Whenever the rainbow appears in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenant" (v.16). The covenant sign is God's own reminder to himself. This is theological condescension of the highest order: God accommodates himself to the human need for visible signs, and in the process commits himself publicly and permanently.

refs.note: As in the days of Noah when I swore the waters would never again cover the earth, so now I have sworn not to be angry with you — Isaiah uses the Noahic covenant as the basis for God's commitment to Israel after judgment.
A rainbow that shone like an emerald encircled the throne — the rainbow reappears around the divine throne, its covenant significance intact.
The appearance of a rainbow... was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD — the rainbow as a theophanic sign.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Noahic covenant is the first universal covenant in Scripture — it encompasses Noah, his descendants, and every living creature. Sarna notes three features: (1) It is entirely unilateral — God commits without requiring anything in return (contrast the Mosaic covenant's bilateral structure). (2) It is universal — all flesh, not just Israel. (3) It is everlasting (berith olam). The rainbow (qeshet) is the covenant sign — a weapon of war hung in the sky as a sign of peace.

The rainbow as covenant sign: Sarna observes that in ancient Near Eastern imagery, the bow (qeshet) was God's weapon. The storm and the divine bow were associated — the storm god wielded the bow-weapon. By hanging the bow in the cloud, God is literally "retiring" his weapon of judgment, placing it in the sky as a sign that the war against creation is over. The rainbow is a disarmed weapon of divine peace.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The threefold repetition of "never again" in vv.11,15 is the structural spine of the covenant. Alter: "The covenant is an unconditional divine promise — the emphatic repetition of 'never again' reflects the gravity of what is being promised. God is binding himself, limiting his own freedom to judge, for the sake of the world he loves. The Noahic covenant is God's permanent commitment to the continued existence of creation."

The rainbow as sign is unique among covenant signs in that it is visible to God ("I will see it and remember") as much as to humans. Alter: "The rainbow is not merely for human reassurance — it is God's own reminder. This is a remarkable theological claim: God institutionalises his own memory, hanging a sign in the sky to remind himself of his covenant commitment."

The rainbow as covenant sign accommodates divine condescension to human weakness. Calvin: "Although the Lord in setting his bow in the clouds, accommodates himself to the capacity of the human mind, yet there is nothing absurd in his using as signs those things which he has already created." The rainbow was already a natural phenomenon; God infuses it with covenantal meaning, turning a meteorological event into a theological sign.

"I will remember my covenant." Calvin: "God does not set his bow in the cloud as if he needed a memorial to prevent forgetfulness; but he has respect to our ignorance and infirmity." The divine memory of the covenant is expressed in terms of human psychological categories — for our sake, not because God forgets.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on qeshet (rainbow/bow) discusses the double meaning: a bow as a weapon and a rainbow as a natural phenomenon. The note favours the view that both meanings are active — the warrior's bow hung in the sky as a sign of peace, a disarmed weapon. The note also observes that v.14 specifies "when I bring clouds over the earth" — the rainbow appears specifically in the storm clouds, the very context of the flood, transforming the ominous storm into a sign of covenant faithfulness.

The NET note on the triple 'my covenant' in vv.15-17 notes the repetition emphasises the unconditional, unilateral character of the Noahic covenant. God is both the initiating party and the binding party. The rainbow is God's own self-reminder, not a human obligation marker.

sections.header: Verses 18–29 — Noah's Drunkenness; the Curse of Canaan

poi.name: Canaan
poi.coords: Coastal and inland Levant; modern Israel/Lebanon/Palestine
poi.text: The curse on Canaan places the narrative in direct relationship to Israel's later history. Canaan is the territory that Israel will enter under Joshua. The Canaanites are identified here as the descendants of a cursed line — the theological groundwork for the conquest narratives.

notes.note: MacArthur: Noah's drunkenness is the first recorded sin after the Flood, illustrating that the new world carries the old problem. Ham's response -- seeing his father's nakedness and telling his brothers -- violates the basic obligation to protect parental dignity. Shem and Japheth's backward walk to cover Noah demonstrates honour.

MacArthur: Noah's curse of Canaan (not Ham) and blessing of Shem and Japheth is prophetic. The curse on Canaan explains the later Canaanites' degraded character and Israel's mandate to displace them. The blessing on Shem anticipates the Semitic line through which the Messiah will come.

heb.paragraph: Noah's drunkenness (v.21) is the first use of yayin in Scripture — and immediately disastrous. The vineyard that Noah plants (v.20) is the first agricultural act after the Flood, carrying both the blessing of renewed cultivation and the danger of what fermentation can do to human judgment. Wine in the OT is simultaneously a symbol of covenant joy (Ps 104:15; John 2:1-11) and of abuse that diminishes the Imago Dei. The scene functions as a typological warning at the dawn of the post-Flood world.

Noah's curse on Canaan (v.25 — "Cursed be Canaan! The lowest of slaves will he be to his brothers") uses the same ʾārûr formula as God's curse on the serpent (3:14) and on Cain (4:11). The curse of Canaan functions in the narrative as a theological explanation for Israel's conquest of Canaan — Canaan's subjugation is not conquest for conquest's sake but the working out of a curse rooted in the moral disorder Ham displayed. The blessing-curse pattern from 1:28 onward structures the entire human story.

"Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem!" (v.26) — the name Shem means "name," and in blessing the God of Shem Noah is blessing the One whose name will be particularly associated with Shem's lineage. The Semitic peoples (named from Shem) carry the covenant line from Noah through Abraham to Israel. The blessing of Japheth (v.27 — "may he dwell in the tents of Shem") is read by later interpreters as the inclusion of the nations (Gentiles) in Israel's covenant inheritance — the universal mission of Gen 12:3.

hist.historical: The "curse of Ham" (actually the curse on Canaan) was notoriously used to justify African slavery from the 17th-19th centuries, based on the racist identification of Ham with dark-skinned Africans. This interpretation has no basis in the text: the curse falls on Canaan (not Ham), concerns Canaanite-Israelite relations (not African-European), and involves no reference to skin colour or race. It is one of the most egregious misreadings of Scripture in history.
hist.context: The drunkenness of Noah is a sobering narrative placed immediately after the greatest covenant in the primeval history. The covenant with all creation is followed by the failure of the covenant's representative. The pattern is consistent: Adam and Eve in the garden, Cain after the fall, the generation before the flood, and now Noah after the flood. Every fresh start in Genesis is followed quickly by fresh failure. The narrative insists that no human covenant partner is adequate — the covenant requires a different kind of mediator.

refs.note: Their eyes were opened and they saw they were naked — the parallel between Noah's nakedness and Adam's.
The Levitical laws against uncovering nakedness — the legal codification of the shame Ham violated.
Let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall — the principle Noah's failure illustrates.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Noah-Ham episode is one of the most debated passages in Genesis. Sarna surveys the main interpretive options for "saw his father's nakedness": (1) literally saw Noah naked; (2) a sexual act (supported by the Leviticus usage of "uncover nakedness"); (3) castration (a rabbinic reading). Sarna notes that whatever the act, Ham's sin was publicising his father's shame rather than covering it. The contrast with Shem and Japheth — walking backward to avoid seeing — shows the proper response.

The curse of Canaan (not Ham) has been the most misused verse in biblical history — wrongly applied to justify African slavery for centuries. Sarna is emphatic: "The curse falls on Canaan, not on Ham, and certainly not on African peoples. The Table of Nations in chapter 10 locates Canaan in the ancient Near East. The curse is a historical prophecy about the Canaanite nations whom Israel will encounter and displace — it has no ethnic application beyond that context."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The narrative economy of the Ham episode is striking. Alter: "Noah is drunk, naked, exposed — a fallen figure, diminished from the man who walked with God and built the ark. Ham sees and tells his brothers. Shem and Japheth walk backward, covering their father. The contrasting actions define two moral stances: Ham the voyeur and publiciser of shame; Shem and Japheth the preservers of dignity."

Shem's blessing ("Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem") is the first instance of blessing directed at a God named in connection with a specific people. Alter: "The blessing of Shem is the theological seed of the Abrahamic narrative that follows. Shem is the ancestor of Abraham; the LORD who will be Abraham's God is here first named as 'the God of Shem.' The primeval history ends by pointing toward the patriarchal history."

Calvin on Ham: "Ham, by bringing his father into derision, violated the most sacred bond of nature. He not only failed to cover the fault of his father, but wished it to be noticed by his brethren." The sin is not seeing but publicising — gossip and dishonour within the family. The covering by Shem and Japheth is the model of godly behaviour: they protect dignity rather than exploit shame.

Calvin on the curse of Canaan: the curse is a prophetic declaration about the historical role of the Canaanite peoples — their subjugation to Israel as God's judgment. Calvin explicitly rejects any racial application: "This is not a curse upon the whole posterity of Ham, but only upon the Canaanites." The misuse of this verse to justify slavery was, in Calvin's reading, a straightforward misreading of the text.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "saw his father's nakedness" discusses the interpretive options (accidental viewing vs. sexual act) and concludes that the ambiguity is intentional — the text is deliberately understated about what exactly Ham did. The note observes that the phrase "uncovering nakedness" in Leviticus is a euphemism for sexual sin, but that the context here does not require that reading. The sin may simply be the failure to honour a parent.

The NET note is explicit about the misuse of the Canaan curse: it applies to the Canaanite peoples of the second millennium BC whom Israel displaced, not to any modern ethnic group. The note provides extensive cross-references to the conquest narratives of Joshua, where the subjugation of Canaan is the historical fulfilment of this curse. The note condemns the historical use of this verse to justify racial hierarchy.

ppl.name: Noah
ppl.role: Covenant mediator of the restored world; first to fail it
ppl.text: The Noahic covenant establishes Noah as the mediator of the new creation — recipient of the most universal covenant in Scripture. His subsequent drunkenness and the shame it exposes follows the pattern of every biblical covenant representative: the human mediator fails, pointing forward to the need for a perfect covenant keeper. He is simultaneously the greatest figure of the primeval history and a demonstration of its limits.

ppl.name: Shem
ppl.role: Father of the Semitic line; bearer of the covenant blessing
ppl.text: Noah's blessing on Shem — "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem" — identifies the Shemite line as the covenant line. From Shem to Abraham (11:10-26) traces the genealogy of the promised seed. Shem's honour of his father contrasts with Ham's violation; his covenant line will run through the entire patriarchal history to the Messiah.

ppl.name: Ham and Canaan
ppl.role: Father of the Canaanite nations; his line under judgment
ppl.text: Ham's sin of dishonoring his father is visited on his son Canaan — establishing the theological basis for the Israelite occupation of Canaan. The Canaanites become the representative people of the kind of moral corruption that characterises the pre-flood world: the curse of Canaan is the theological counterpart of the judgment that preceded the flood.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslation9:6 — the chiastic structureMT structureשֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם [A:blood / B:of man] / בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ [B:by man / A:his blood shall be shed]TranslationWhoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shedNoteThe chiasm (A-B / B-A) structurally embodies the lex talionis — the punishment mirrors the crime. The basis: "for in the image of God has God made mankind." The imago Dei is the foundation of both the prohibition and the sanction.9:13 — qeshet (bow)NIV/ESVI have set my rainbow in the cloudsNoteQeshet is the standard Hebrew word for a war bow (used 76 times, almost always of a weapon). The "rainbow" reading is a natural inference from the context (clouds, rain) rather than a specific Hebrew word. The war-bow interpretation sees God laying down his weapon of judgment.9:6 — the chiastic structureMT structureשֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם [A:blood / B:of man] / בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ [B:by man / A:his blood shall be shed]TranslationWhoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shedNoteThe chiasm (A-B / B-A) structurally embodies the lex talionis — the punishment mirrors the crime. The basis: "for in the image of God has God made mankind." The imago Dei is the foundation of both the prohibition and the sanction.9:13 — qeshet (bow)NIV/ESVI have set my rainbow in the cloudsNoteQeshet is the standard Hebrew word for a war bow (used 76 times, almost always of a weapon). The "rainbow" reading is a natural inference from the context (clouds, rain) rather than a specific Hebrew word. The war-bow interpretation sees God laying down his weapon of judgment.NIVWhoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed; for in the image of God has God made mankindESVWhoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own imageNASBWhoever sheds man's blood, by man his blood shall be shed, for in the image of God He made manNoteThis verse is rendered in Hebrew as a chiasm: shofekh dam ha-adam / ba-adam damo yishafekh — 'one who sheds blood of the human / by the human his blood shall be shed.' The literary mirroring enacts the principle of proportional justice. The grounding ('for God made man in his image') makes this the most theologically weighty capital punishment argument in Scripture — and simultaneously limits it: not all killing, but killing that violates the image of God.NIVWhenever the rainbow appears in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenantESVWhen the bow is in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenantNASBwhen the bow is in the cloud, then I will look upon it, to remember the everlasting covenantNoteBerit olam — everlasting/eternal covenant. The rainbow is a sign for God to see ('I will see it') — not primarily a display for humanity. God reminds himself of his promise every time it rains. This is a remarkable statement of divine self-commitment: God binds himself to a sign of his own keeping. The bow is also the Hebrew word for a warrior's bow (qeshet) — the divine warrior hangs up his war-bow, a symbol of ceasefire.NIVCursed be Canaan! The lowest of slaves will he be to his brothersESVCursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be to his brothersNASBCursed be Canaan; a servant of servants he shall be to his brothersNoteAll translations render this similarly. The 'curse of Ham' is a historical misnomer — the text curses Canaan, not Ham, and Ham's other sons (Cush, Egypt, Put) are not cursed. The horrific use of this verse to justify African slavery (beginning with European colonialism in the 16th c.) represents one of the worst abuses of biblical interpretation in history. The verse concerns Israel's relationship to Canaanite peoples in the conquest period, not any racial group.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Meals
src.quote: In the ANE, covenants were typically ratified by a shared meal — the two parties eating together as a sign of mutual commitment. Noah's sacrifice and God's acceptance of the pleasing aroma (8:20-21) functions as the covenant meal of the Noahic covenant: God and his covenant partner share in the offering. The rainbow covenant formalises what the sacrifice established.
src.note: The ANE covenant-meal parallel illuminates the structure of the flood narrative: sacrifice (8:20-21) → covenant promise (8:22) → formal covenant ratification with sign (9:8-17). The pattern is followed in the Abrahamic covenant (15:9-21) and the Sinai covenant (24:9-11).

src.title: The Ugaritic Baal Cycle and the Warrior's Bow
src.quote: In Canaanite mythology, the storm god Baal defeats the sea god Yam with a pair of magical clubs. The storm god's weapons are the instruments of cosmic victory. The placement of the divine bow in the clouds after the flood has been compared to the warrior god hanging up his weapons after victory.
src.note: The comparison illuminates the ANE background of qeshet (bow) in a storm-theophany context, but inverts the mythology: in the Baal Cycle, the storm god wins; in Genesis, the same God who controlled the storm now disarms himself as a sign of covenant peace. The Genesis account is not dependent on the Baal mythology but engages its imagery deliberately.

rec.text: The Noahic covenant became the foundation for the Reformed doctrine of covenant theology, which reads all of Scripture through the lens of successive covenants (creation, Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, new). The Noahic covenant functions in this system as the covenant of "common grace" — God's universal provision for all humanity that creates the conditions within which the covenant of redemption can operate.

Aquinas discusses the natural law basis of the Noahic covenant's prohibitions (blood-eating, murder) — arguing these are expressions of natural law knowable to all humans, codified in the Noahic covenant before the Mosaic law. The Noahic covenant is thus the basis for Aquinas's claim that moral obligations are universal, not limited to Israel.

Kuyper's multi-volume work develops the theological concept of common grace rooted in the Noahic covenant. God's commitment to maintain the world for all people, regardless of their relationship to him, is the basis for human culture, science, art, and civilisation. The Noahic covenant is not merely about survival — it is the covenantal foundation for the goodness of creation order that continues to operate in a fallen world.

rows.label: The Rainbow: God Disarms Himself
rows.text: The bow (qeshet -- also a warrior's bow) is placed in the cloud pointing upward. God symbolically disarms himself as the covenant sign. The rainbow is for God's own remembrance, not humanity's reminder.

rows.label: Noah's Curse and Blessing
rows.text: Ham sees and tells; Shem and Japheth walk backward to cover. The blessing follows protective modesty; the curse follows exploitative disclosure. The table of moral responses will shape the subsequent Table of Nations.

hebtext.note: "God blessed Noah and his sons" (v.1) deliberately echoes the creation blessing of 1:28: "God blessed them and said, 'Be fruitful and multiply.'" The Noahic covenant is a re-creation covenant — the blessing formula is the signal that the creation mandate is being re-issued. Every element of the first blessing reappears: fruitfulness, multiplication, dominion over animals. The Flood was not the end of creation but its purgation and renewal.

The new permission to eat meat (v.3) is immediately qualified by the prohibition on consuming blood (v.4: "you must not eat meat that has its lifeblood still in it"). Blood (dam) in biblical law carries the life of the creature (nefesh — soul/life, v.4) — to eat blood would be to consume life itself, which belongs to God. This is the theological foundation of all subsequent blood legislation: Leviticus 17:11 — "the life of a creature is in the blood."

The prohibition on murder grounds itself in the Imago Dei (v.6): "Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed; for in the image of God has God made mankind." The image of God, transmitted through the genealogy of ch.5, is the basis of human dignity and the ground of the legal prohibition on homicide. Capital justice is not revenge but the defence of the Imago: to murder a person is to attack the image-bearer of God.

The Noahic covenant (vv.9-17) is the first formal covenant in Scripture, using the word bĕrît for the first time in Genesis. God initiates it unilaterally — not "if you do X, I will do Y" but an unconditional divine commitment to sustain the created order. The covenant is universal: with Noah, his descendants (all humanity), and every living creature. It is the covenant of common grace that underlies all subsequent history, including Israel's particular covenant and the new covenant.

The rainbow (qešet) as covenant sign (vv.12-17) is a warfare bow hung in the clouds — weapons of war set aside as a peace signal. When God looks at the bow, he will "remember my everlasting covenant between God and all living creatures" (v.16). The sign is oriented primarily toward God's own remembering, not humanity's: it is a reminder built into the creation of divine commitment, permanently marking the sky with the shape of a covenant.

The Noahic covenant is characterised as bĕrît ʿôlām — "everlasting covenant" (v.16). This is the first of four such "everlasting covenants" in Genesis-Deuteronomy (also Abrahamic: 17:7; Sabbath: Exod 31:16; Levitical: Num 25:13). The ʿôlām qualifier signals permanence that transcends any political arrangement — it is a commitment as durable as creation itself, grounded in God's own character rather than in human performance.

Noah's drunkenness (v.21) is the first use of yayin in Scripture — and immediately disastrous. The vineyard that Noah plants (v.20) is the first agricultural act after the Flood, carrying both the blessing of renewed cultivation and the danger of what fermentation can do to human judgment. Wine in the OT is simultaneously a symbol of covenant joy (Ps 104:15; John 2:1-11) and of abuse that diminishes the Imago Dei. The scene functions as a typological warning at the dawn of the post-Flood world.

Noah's curse on Canaan (v.25 — "Cursed be Canaan! The lowest of slaves will he be to his brothers") uses the same ʾārûr formula as God's curse on the serpent (3:14) and on Cain (4:11). The curse of Canaan functions in the narrative as a theological explanation for Israel's conquest of Canaan — Canaan's subjugation is not conquest for conquest's sake but the working out of a curse rooted in the moral disorder Ham displayed. The blessing-curse pattern from 1:28 onward structures the entire human story.

"Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem!" (v.26) — the name Shem means "name," and in blessing the God of Shem Noah is blessing the One whose name will be particularly associated with Shem's lineage. The Semitic peoples (named from Shem) carry the covenant line from Noah through Abraham to Israel. The blessing of Japheth (v.27 — "may he dwell in the tents of Shem") is read by later interpreters as the inclusion of the nations (Gentiles) in Israel's covenant inheritance — the universal mission of Gen 12:3.

tx.title: Noah's curse — euphemistic language
tx.content: MT reads 'saw the nakedness of his father' (wayyarʾ ʾet-ʿerwat ʾābîw). The LXX translates literally (εἶδεν τὴν γύμνωσιν τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ). However, Targum Onqelos and several rabbinic sources imply the act was more than visual — some traditions read castration or sexual violation. The text itself is deliberately ambiguous.
tx.note: The MT's euphemistic restraint is itself a textual datum: the narrator chose opaque language, and later traditions filled the gap in contradictory ways.

tx.title: Japheth dwelling in the tents of Shem
tx.content: MT reads 'may God enlarge Japheth, and may he dwell in the tents of Shem' — the subject of 'dwell' is ambiguous (God or Japheth?). LXX resolves it toward Japheth (κατοικησάτω). Targum Onqelos reads it as God's Shekinah dwelling among Shem's descendants.
tx.note: The Targum's theological reading became normative in rabbinic Judaism. The Christian tradition typically read Japheth (Gentiles) joining Shem's tents as a prophecy of Gentile inclusion.

debate.title: Ham's Sin: What Did He Do?

positions.name: Observed nakedness only
positions.argument: Ham saw his father's nakedness (a shameful sight in ancient Near Eastern culture) and reported it to his brothers with evident delight rather than covering it respectfully as Shem and Japheth did. The sin is disrespect for paternal dignity.

positions.name: Castration
positions.argument: Ham castrated Noah while he slept — which is why Noah says "Canaan will be the lowest of slaves" (depriving Canaan of position). The evidence: Noah's response implies something more than voyeurism.

positions.name: Incest with Noah's wife
positions.argument: "Uncovering a father's nakedness" is a euphemism for sexual relations with the father's wife (cf. Lev 18:7-8). Ham slept with Noah's wife and Canaan was the offspring.

positions.name: Ritual humiliation
positions.argument: Ham's act had ritual or cultural dimensions — mocking the father who lay naked was a formal act of disrespect with social/legal implications in ancient Near Eastern contexts.

positions.name: Prophetic
positions.argument: Noah speaks as a prophet, pronouncing the future destiny of the three major ethnic divisions of humanity. The cursed servitude of Canaan will be fulfilled in Israel's conquest under Joshua.

positions.name: Etiological
positions.argument: The story explains (provides an etiology for) why Canaanites are subordinate to Israelites — a backward-looking justification written during or after the conquest period.

positions.name: Both
positions.argument: The prophecy is historically grounded and forward-looking while also providing a theological framework for understanding Israel's later relationship with Canaan.

debate.title: The Curse of Canaan: Prophecy or Justification?

positions.name: Prophetic
positions.argument: Noah speaks as a prophet, pronouncing the future destiny of the three major ethnic divisions of humanity. The cursed servitude of Canaan will be fulfilled in Israel's conquest under Joshua.

positions.name: Etiological
positions.argument: The story explains (provides an etiology for) why Canaanites are subordinate to Israelites — a backward-looking justification written during or after the conquest period.

positions.name: Both
positions.argument: The prophecy is historically grounded and forward-looking while also providing a theological framework for understanding Israel's later relationship with Canaan.

coaching.tip: God's commission to Noah — 'Be fruitful and multiply' — deliberately echoes Genesis 1:28. But there is a significant addition: 'the fear and dread of you will fall on all the beasts.' The post-flood world is recognizably the same creation, but the relationship between humanity and nature has fundamentally changed.

The curse on Canaan (not Ham directly) has been catastrophically misused throughout history to justify slavery and racism. The text itself is narrowly focused: it anticipates Israel's later conflict with the Canaanite nations. Reading it as a statement about race imports a framework the author never intended.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on God's covenant promise never to destroy the earth again by flood, consider asking the Lord to help you see the signs of His faithfulness in your own life — the rainbows after your storms — and to trust that His promises stand even when the sky darkens.
